original_reasoning: Arabic التفن: filth/dirt. Greek τίφινος: of einkorn wheat. Dirt and grain chaff are both fine particulate matter from threshing — possible mafahim connection through agricultural waste.

Arabic التليث: a type of plant from saline ground. Greek τελέθω: to come around (of a season). Seasonal plants that return/bloom — the plant's appearance marks the season's coming. Plausible mafahim link.

Arabic الثلج: snow, also 'heart settled/calmed' (ثَلَجَتْ نفسي = my soul found peace). Greek θέλγω: to charm/enchant/bewitch. Both express a calming/stilling of the soul — snow cools and calms, charm bewitches and stills. Mafahim connection through 'stilling the soul.'

Arabic الجلبار: scabbard of a sword or its edge. Greek Γαλάβριοι: ancient Illyrian tribe in Kosovo. Tribe name and sword-scabbard — if the tribe were known as weapon-carriers, possible ethnonym from weapon-term. Speculative.

Arabic الحتر: tightening/fastening, also feeding (اطعام), also a small amount. Greek χύτρα: earthen pot for boiling. Pots for cooking/feeding — the Arabic 'feeding' sense connects to Greek cooking vessel. Faint mafahim.

Arabic الازفل = anger and sharpness (الغضب والحدة); also a crowd/group (الأزفلى). Greek ἐπιζάφελος = vehement, violent, furious (intensified form of ζάφελος). Both Arabic الغضب/حدة and Greek ἐπιζάφελος share a core of intense violent force/fury. The Arabic lexical form (ازفل) may relate to the same Semitic-Greek contact zone that produced this intensity vocabulary.
Arabic: deadly poison (the lethal substance). Greek: oleander (a poisonous plant). Both sit in the semantic field of deadly/toxic substances. Plausible mafahim link: poison-carrying plant.

Arabic: large saddle; also a woman who loses consciousness during intercourse. Greek: dodder (Cuscuta) — a parasitic plant that strangles/chokes other plants. Both masadiq senses: heavy attachment/parasitic clinging. Plausible plant-adjacent connection.
